text: In this video, we'll be describing a patient who was having grade four rectal prolapse. The surgery involves fixation of a mesh on the posterior aspect of the rectum and anchoring it to the sacral promontory.
The surgeon begins the rectopexy procedure by mobilizing the sigmoid colon, ensuring adequate bowel mobilization for subsequent steps.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum at the sacral promontory, carefully lateralizing the ureter and dissecting the peritoneum down to the level of the uterosacral ligament. The same procedure is performed on the opposite side, with attention to preserving the inferior hypogastric nerve, located approximately 1 to 1.5 centimeters medial to the ureter.

The surgeon applies traction to the rectum, pushing it cranially to open the presacral space. Gentle and blunt dissection is used to expose the presacral space, taking care to avoid the venous plexus to prevent bleeding. The rectum is mobilized, and the presacral space is opened up to the level of the coccyx bone.

The surgeon begins lateral mobilization of the rectum after completing posterior mobilization, carefully preserving the inferior hypogastric nerve and its branches near the middle rectal artery to maintain post-operative rectal function.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the rectum on the posterior aspect, opens the pouch of Douglas, and resects the Denon-Wheeler fascia. The rectum is fully mobilized to the level of the anal canal. The pubococcygeus muscle and fascia are visible, along with the puborectalis muscle, which is exposed for rectal prolapse repair. The inferior hypogastric nerve is preserved on both sides, ensuring nerve-sparing prolapse repair.

The surgeon dissects open the anterior longitudinal ligament over the sacral promontory, preparing the area for mesh anchoring. A standard mesh is inserted and anchored onto the presacral fascia using 3-0 ester sutures.

The surgeon anchors the mesh along both sides of the puborectalis muscle, taking deep bites at this level for extra anchorage. This technique is similar to the correction of rectocele, where the mesh is anchored to the posterior aspect of the vagina.

The surgeon anchors the posterior aspect of the rectum and surrounding pararectal fat to the mesh, ensuring it remains firm over the posterior rectum. The rectum is then pulled up and anchored to the sacral promontory, with care taken to avoid excessive tension on the mesh to prevent post-operative complications.
The surgeon places two tackers at the sacral promontory to secure the mesh, which is then reperitonealized. Adequate traction is maintained on the mesh without applying excessive force.
